avoid: Very long flat hair
Heavy long fringe that covers forehead
Overly voluminous styles at the sides
Center parting with flat lifeless hair
Too much layering at the sides
Over-gelled stiff styles that hide natural texture
Excessive width at the sides with thick hair
oval.notes: Oval is the most versatile face shape — nearly any hairstyle works. The balanced proportions (slightly longer than wide) mean you can experiment freely. Key barber advice: use matte products like clays and pastes for natural texture; add subtle height at the front for structure; keep the sides slightly shorter to maintain clean lines. Avoid styles that add too much volume on the sides (making face appear wider) or very long heavy fringe (making face appear shorter and rounder). The golden rule is to let the haircut follow the natural flow of the head, not work against it. Beard pairing: short fade + stubble for a sharp look, or textured top + full beard for a rugged aesthetic.

recommended: High Fade with Volume on Top
avoid: Buzz cut
Caesar Cut
Full sides with no taper
Straight blunt fringe
Rounded bowl cuts
Crew cut without height
Center part without volume
Low fades that emphasize width
Curly hair worn flat without height
Overly rounded beard styles
round.notes: Round faces have similar width and length with softer jawlines and full cheeks. The goal is to create the illusion of structure and angles by adding height on top and keeping sides tight. Key barber advice: high fades are the best option — they create vertical emphasis making the face appear more oval. Avoid low fades which make the face look rounder. Square corners at the high recession area sharpen up soft edges. Always opt for volume and lift at the crown. Use volumizing products, blow-dry upward for lift. Strategic facial hair helps: a beard with length at the chin creates a V-shape that elongates the face — avoid full rounded beards. Best fade pairings: high fade + pompadour, high fade + quiff, high fade + textured crop with lift.

avoid: Very rounded styles that add width
Boxy sides with too much width
Very flat top styles that emphasize squareness
Center parting that highlights symmetry
Overly structured geometric cuts
Crew cut that's too blunt and boxy
square.notes: Square faces feature a strong jawline, broad forehead, and angular lines — considered very masculine. Most men prefer to soften the angles for a less intense look. Key barber advice: the asymmetry of a side part breaks up the perceived uniformity and makes things look more interesting. Greater length on top creates the illusion of height to offset the square shape. An angled fringe draws the eye away from the jawline. You can go two ways: keep it short and cropped (military buzz cut) for an alpha look, OR add length and weight around the face with a high quiff or textured side parting to soften angles. For beards: go very short (stubble) or fairly long — medium length can look awkward. Keep sideburns short. The emphasis is on creating contrasting vertical lengths either on top or with a beard.

recommended: Low Fade with Volume on Top
avoid: Slicked back styles
High volume pompadour
Very short sides with no volume at jaw level
Buzz cut that exposes wide forehead
Styles that add excessive volume at the top only
Tight pulled-back styles that emphasize forehead width
Center parting that highlights the V-shape
heart.notes: Heart-shaped faces have a wider forehead tapering to a narrower chin — the goal is to balance the top-heavy proportions. Key barber advice: add volume and width at the chin level, not just the top. Medium-length styles work well as they add width around the jaw. Side-swept bangs help reduce forehead prominence. A medium-length textured style with some width at the sides creates the best balance. Use products that give texture and movement rather than height. Avoid anything that adds more volume to the top of the head (exaggerates the V-shape). Taper fades and undercuts work well when paired with sufficient length on top swept to the side. Crew cuts and butch cuts also work for a low-maintenance professional look. Facial hair: a fuller beard at the chin area helps balance the narrower jaw.

avoid: Pompadour
Very tall styles with excessive height
Very tight sides with long top (skin fades)
Tapered faux hawks with deep fades
High top knots
Straight flat hair without volume at the sides
Mohawk-style cuts that elongate the face
Overly long slicked-back styles
oblong.notes: Oblong faces are noticeably longer than wide. The goal is to add WIDTH and volume at the sides while avoiding any additional height. Key barber advice: never go too short on the sides — a high fade can make an oblong face appear narrower. Retain some length on the sides. Use matte products to keep attention on the shape, not shine. Don't hide natural wave — wavy texture gives volume at the sides which is exactly what you need. A Caesar cut or textured crop brings hair forward to cover the forehead, creating a shortening effect. For medium-length styles, curtain bangs create a face-framing effect adding width. When wearing hair long, avoid straight cuts that pull the face downward — go for shoulder-length with layers. Wear long hair in a low bun or ponytail at the base of the neck, never a high top knot. Keep any slicked-back style flat or slightly angled to one side.

avoid: Buzz cut without any length on top
Very short cropped all-over styles
Straight back slicked styles that expose all angles
Styles that add too much width at the cheekbones
Flat lifeless hair with no texture
Overly boxy or geometric cuts
Center parting that highlights the narrow forehead
diamond.notes: Diamond faces are characterized by narrow jawline and forehead with width at the cheekbones — very angular and masculine. The goal is to add width at the forehead and soften the sharpness of the jawline. Key barber advice: opt for layered cuts with strong, sweeping sections. A messy fringe paired with a high fade makes for a great contemporary option — the fringe adds texture to the forehead while the fade keeps it clean. Keep the hair relatively long and layered with soft angles to play off the pointy features. The trick is to add loads of texture to break up hard lines and add movement. High fades with sweeping fringes add width to the top while accentuating cheekbones. Faux hawks work well but don't style too high or it becomes boxy. Natural wavy texture works beautifully — let it dry naturally or use a diffuser. Use styling cream for volume and texture or high-shine pomade for a glossy finish.

recommended: Low Fade with Volume on Top
avoid: Very short buzz cuts that expose the jawline
Tight skin fades on the sides
Slicked back styles that expose the wide jaw
Flat top cuts that narrow the forehead
Styles with no volume at the temples
triangle.notes: Triangle face shape is the inverse of heart — wider at the jawline and narrower at the forehead. The goal is to add width and volume at the forehead and temples to balance the wider jaw. Key barber advice: keep the hair close to the head with no extra volume near the ears, and gradually increase width at the temple and forehead area. Fringes and textured bangs work exceptionally well to add width at the top. Use volumizing products at the roots near the forehead. Medium-length styles with layers around the cheekbone area help balance proportions. Side-swept styles with volume create asymmetry that softens the jaw width. Avoid pulling hair tight against the head as this emphasizes the jaw-heavy silhouette. The barber should focus on building width in the upper third of the face while keeping the sides tapered.

recommended: Natural Texture with Length on Sides
avoid: Skin fades that make face look thinner and longer
Pompadour with excessive height
High quiffs that elongate the face
Very short all-over cuts
Straight flat hair without texture
Tight slicked-back styles
Overly tall structured styles
rectangle.notes: Rectangle face shape is similar to square but more elongated — forehead, cheekbones and jawline are similar in width but the face is longer. The goal is to add width around the face without adding height. Key barber advice: going super-short or opting for a skin fade will only make the face look thinner and longer — avoid this. Keep length on the sides and add texture to the top. A scissor cut works better than clipper cuts for this shape. For variation, try a side-parting crew cut keeping the sides long and the body messy and texturized. The emphasis is on building horizontal volume rather than vertical height. Use products that create texture and movement — matte clays and sea salt sprays work well. Avoid any style that draws the eye vertically upward. Medium-length cuts with natural movement are the sweet spot.
